One fateful decision 100 years ago created parallel lives. How does a family broken by the bizarre rules of racism heal itself after three generations apart?
When their mother died, Edward and George DeGrange went to live at a New Orleans orphanage for Black children.
Edward eventually headed to Chicago, where he lived as a white man.
George, whose skin was slightly darker, stayed behind in New Orleans.
For the rest of his life, George rarely spoke of his brother Edward. He raised seven children and 30 grandchildren.
Those grandchildren included this reporter. Here’s how an American secret, once a matter of life or death, unfolded across generations.

The Testosterone Moment Is Here, and Men May Never Look the Same
I dove into the world of testosterone, finding that, from politics to influencers and beyond, millions of men are taking the hormone in pursuit of a new masculine ideal.
Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., who extols the president’s testosterone levels and speaks about his own use of the hormone, is working with other health officials to expand access.
Testosterone prescriptions grew to nearly 12 million in 2025, from under 1 million in 2000, rising most among younger men and fueled by clinics promoting it as a lifestyle product.
Figures including Vice President JD Vance and Elon Musk have amplified the message that being “low T” is synonymous with low status, weakness, sexual inadequacy and even liberal politics.
Some men said they emerged from an online rabbit hole convinced their testosterone levels were the key to what ailed them — and that society had lost touch with true masculinity.
